name: italian stuffed peppers
instructions:
TWO VARIANTS. Both cook the bell peppers SEPARATELY from the filling, and both finish with off-heat basil + shredded monterrey jack (or another soft cheese).

VARIANT A — MEAT (OR VEGAN MEAT):
- Sauté finely diced yellow onion and celery in olive oil until softened — this is the savory base.
- Add ground beef (or impossible/vegan ground meat); cook until done, breaking it up.
- Stir in herbes de provence and black pepper. Salt to taste.
- Off heat, fold in torn fresh basil leaves.
- Spoon into the (separately-cooked) bell pepper halves. Top with shredded monterrey jack and broil briefly to melt.

VARIANT B — SEASONED TOFU CRUMBLES (PREFERRED):
- Crumble super firm tofu by hand into rough small chunks.
- Toss the crumbles with potato or corn starch, a heavy pinch of salt, garlic powder, onion powder, paprika, and black pepper.
- Bake at ~425°F until the crumbles are browned and crisp on the outside.
- In a bowl, combine the baked tofu crumbles with FINELY DICED RAW celery (not sautéed — see notes), torn fresh basil leaves, and herbes de provence.
- Spoon into the (separately-cooked) bell pepper halves. Top with shredded monterrey jack and broil briefly to melt.
notes:
TWO VARIANTS, ONE SHARED FRAME: both versions use bell peppers as the boat, cheese as the dairy finish, fresh off-heat basil as the aromatic. The filling protein and the celery treatment are what differ.

BELL PEPPERS COOKED SEPARATELY — DON'T BAKE BASIL INTO THE PEPPER: cook the bell pepper halves on their own (oven or skillet), then add the prepared filling at the end. Baking the basil into the pepper kills the basil — same logic as basil-in-pasta-sauce: it goes off heat at the end, never cooked in.

BASIL OFF HEAT, ALWAYS: torn fresh basil leaves go in last, off heat. This is the single most load-bearing technique note across both variants. Cooked basil reads stale and the dish loses its italian register.

MONTERREY JACK (OR ANY SOFT CHEESE): monterrey jack is the canonical pick — melts cleanly, doesn't compete with the basil. Other soft melty cheeses (mozzarella, fontina, soft provolone) work fine. Hard cheeses (parmesan, aged cheddar) don't — they don't melt right for this format.

=== VARIANT B IS PREFERRED ===

VARIANT B IS BETTER: I prefer the tofu-and-raw-celery variant to the meat one, for two reasons:
- Tofu is cheaper than meat, and the seasoned-and-baked crumbles carry the savory load just as well.
- The raw finely-diced celery makes the texture less monotonous. In variant A, the celery is sautéed into the base alongside the onion and meat — everything ends up the same softened texture. In variant B, the celery stays crunchy and contrasts with the crispy-baked tofu crumbles. Each forkful has two textures (crisp tofu + crunchy celery) instead of one (uniform softened filling).

SEASONED TOFU CRUMBLES — SUPER FIRM, STARCH-COATED, HEAVILY SEASONED, BAKED CRISP: the variant B technique is its own thing worth getting right.
- Use SUPER FIRM tofu, not firm. Super firm holds its shape during the crumble-and-bake without needing to be pressed.
- Crumble by hand into rough small chunks — not pulverized, you want bite-sized pieces.
- Toss with potato starch or corn starch — coats the surface, gives you the crispy-fried texture during the bake.
- HEAVY salt and seasonings — tofu is bland baseline; under-season it and the dish reads flat. Garlic powder, onion powder, paprika, salt, black pepper all on a generous hand.
- Bake hot (~425°F) on a sheet pan until crisp and browned. The starch coating is what crisps.

VARIANT A — SAUTÉED ONION + CELERY + MEAT IS THE SAVORY BACKBONE: in the meat variant, the trio of softened yellow onion, softened celery, and cooked ground meat (real or faux) is the structural filling. The cheese on top adds dairy depth; the basil adds the aromatic finish. The mistake here would be skimping on the onion or undercooking it — both make the filling read flat.

CELERY — DIFFERENT TREATMENT BY VARIANT: variant A wants celery softened into the savory base; variant B wants celery raw and finely diced for textural contrast. Same vegetable, different role. Don't sauté the celery for variant B and don't leave it raw for variant A — each variant's choice is load-bearing for that variant's texture goal.

HERBES DE PROVENCE: works in both variants. Goes into the cook (variant A) or tossed in with the assembly (variant B). It's a background herb register, not a finishing herb — distinct from the off-heat basil's job.
